era_context: The early 14th century is marked by the consolidation of major empires and the beginning of significant institutional changes in Europe. The Papacy is transitioning toward Avignon, the Delhi Sultanate is at its territorial height, the Yuan Dynasty is managing internal factions, and the Ottoman Empire is beginning its rise. The Hundred Years' War is still in the future, but Anglo-French tensions are mounting. The Mali Empire is expanding in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific Islands (minimal written records)
Americas (no written records in this period)

events.title: Mass Arrest of Knights Templar
events.description: On Friday, October 13, 1307, King Philip IV of France orchestrates the simultaneous arrest of Knights Templar throughout France. The order, once a powerful military and financial institution, is accused of heresy, idol worship, and other charges. The arrests are coordinated to prevent the Templars from escaping or hiding their assets. This marks the beginning of the end for the order and represents a major shift in power from religious military institutions to secular monarchies. The event has profound implications for the relationship between church and state in medieval Europe.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay (Grand Master of the Templars)

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles (e.g., Jean de Saint-Victor, Grandes Chroniques de France)

events.certainty_note: The arrests are extensively documented in contemporary French royal records and chronicles. The date of Friday, October 13, 1307, is firmly established.
cross_references: 1308 (trials continue); 1309 (papal relocation and continued trials); 1311–1312 (Council of Vienne formal suppression)

events.title: Clement V Elected Pope
events.description: Clement V (Bertrand de Got) is elected Pope in 1305, but his papacy is formally inaugurated in 1307. His election was heavily influenced by French King Philip IV, marking a significant shift in papal independence. Clement V's papacy is characterized by his close relationship with the French crown and his willingness to accommodate royal interests. His election and early policies set the stage for the relocation of the papal seat to Avignon in 1309.
events.certainty_note: Clement V's election in 1305 and his formal inauguration in 1307 are well-documented. His early papacy is clearly established.

events.title: Wuzong Ascends to Yuan Throne
events.description: Emperor Wuzong (Kulug Khan) ascends to the Yuan throne in 1307, beginning a reign marked by political instability and competing power centers. His accession is contested by rival factions within the Mongol aristocracy and Han Chinese officials. Wuzong's reign (1307–1311) is characterized by attempts to balance Mongol and Han interests, but internal factionalism continues to plague the dynasty.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Yuan political history

events.certainty_note: Wuzong's accession in 1307 is well-established in Yuan historical records, though the circumstances are disputed by some scholars.
cross_references: 1311 (death of Wuzong); relates to broader Yuan Dynasty history

events.title: Delhi Sultanate Under Alauddin Khalji's Late Reign
events.description: Alauddin Khalji continues his reign as Sultan of Delhi, which began in 1296. By 1307, the sultanate is at the height of its territorial extent, controlling much of the Indian subcontinent. Alauddin is known for his military campaigns, administrative reforms, and price controls. However, by 1307, his health is declining, and succession disputes are beginning to emerge. The sultanate's vast territorial holdings are becoming increasingly difficult to manage.
events.certainty_note: Alauddin Khalji's reign is well-established (1296–1316), but specific events in 1307 are not precisely documented. The general political situation is inferred from broader historical context.

events.title: Ottoman Early Expansion Under Osman I
events.description: The Ottoman beylik continues its gradual expansion in northwestern Anatolia under Osman I. By 1307, the Ottomans are consolidating control over former Byzantine and Seljuk territories through military campaigns and strategic alliances. The capture of Bursa (1326) remains in the future, but the foundation for Ottoman expansion is being laid. This period marks the transition from a small principality to a regional power.
events.certainty_note: Early Ottoman history is poorly documented in contemporary sources. Most accounts come from later Ottoman chronicles. The specific date of 1307 is not precisely marked.
cross_references: 1326 (capture of Bursa); relates to broader Ottoman rise

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the Knights Templar arrests as a purely political conspiracy by Philip IV to seize wealth has been complicated by recent scholarship suggesting genuine concerns about the order's practices and independence. Some historians argue that the charges of heresy, while likely exaggerated or fabricated, may have had some basis in the order's secretive rituals. Additionally, the characterization of the event as marking the 'end' of the Templars is somewhat misleading, as the order continued in some form in other regions (notably Portugal) after 1312. The dating of early Ottoman expansion is highly uncertain; some sources place key events several years earlier or later than traditionally assigned.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1307 is heavily weighted toward Western European sources, particularly French royal records and papal documents. The arrest of the Templars is one of the best-documented events of the year, with extensive contemporary records. Asian sources are more sparse and often compiled centuries after the events. African and American histories are almost entirely absent from contemporary written records. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites—court chronicles, religious institutions, and merchant records. Oral traditions and non-elite perspectives are largely invisible. The year 1307 is well-documented for Western Europe but only moderately documented for Asia and poorly documented for most other regions.

graph_edges.note: The arrests of 1307 lead to trials and interrogations in 1308
The Templar suppression continues from 1307 through 1309 and beyond
Clement V's papacy in 1307 leads to the relocation to Avignon in 1309
Wuzong's reign from 1307 to 1311 is marked by internal factionalism
Alauddin Khalji's late reign in 1307 leads to succession disputes and decline after 1316
